author: Katy Hagerty

title: Interpretability and implicit model semantics in biomedicine and deep learning
content:
Nature Machine Intelligence, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s42256-026-01177-0

We introduce a framework to analyse interpretability in deep learning, by drawing on a formal notion of model semantics from the philosophy of science. We argue that interpretability is only one aspect of a model’s semantics and illustrate our framework with examples from biomedicine.
author: Mark Gerstein

title: Computational framework to predict and shape human–machine interactions in closed-loop, co-adaptive neural interfaces
content:
Nature Machine Intelligence, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s42256-026-01194-z

Madduri et al. introduce a computational framework grounded in control and game theory to model co-adaptation between users and decoders in neural interfaces. This framework enables a principled design of closed-loop systems that improve usability and personalization.
author: Amy L. Orsborn

title: Deep Learning for Clouds and Cloud Shadow Segmentation in Methane Satellite and Airborne Imaging Spectroscopy
content: Effective cloud and cloud shadow detection is a critical prerequisite for accurate retrieval of concentrations of atmospheric methane (CH4) or other trace gases in hyperspectral remote sensing. This challenge is especially pertinent for MethaneSAT, a satellite mission launched in March 2024, to fill a significant data gap in terms of resolution, precision, and swath between coarse-resolution global mappers and fine-scale point-source imagers of methane, and for its airborne companion mission, MethaneAIR. MethaneSAT delivers hyperspectral data at an intermediate spatial resolution ( $\sim{\!}{\!} 100 \times 400$  m), whereas MethaneAIR provides even finer resolution (~25 m), enabling the development of highly detailed maps of concentrations that enable quantification of both the sources and rates of emissions. In this study, we use machine learning methods to address the cloud and cloud shadow detection problem for sensors with these high spatial resolutions. Cloud and cloud shadows in remote sensing data need to be effectively screened out as they bias methane retrievals in remote sensing imagery and impact the quantification of emissions. We deploy and evaluate conventional techniques—including iterative logistic regression (ILR) and multilayer perceptron (MLP)—with advanced deep learning architectures, namely, U-Net and a spectral channel attention network (SCAN) method. Our results show that conventional methods struggle with spatial coherence and boundary definition, affecting the detection of clouds and cloud shadows. Deep learning models substantially improve detection quality: U-Net performs best in preserving spatial structure, while SCAN excels at capturing fine boundary details. Notably, SCAN surpasses U-Net on MethaneSAT data, underscoring the benefits of incorporating spectral attention for satellite-specific features. In addition, we combine the predictions of both U-Net and SCAN through a convolutional neural network (CNN). This ensemble method achieves the best performance on both MethaneAIR ( $F1$ :  $78.50~\pm ~3.08$ %) and MethaneSAT ( $F1$ :  $78.80~\pm ~1.28$ %) datasets, representing improvements of 2% and 10% over conventional methods (U-Net:  $78.50~\pm ~3.08$ % and  $68.56~\pm ~0.36$ %  $F1$ , respectively), while maintaining efficient inference (4.1 ms per 1000 km2). This in-depth assessment of various disparate machine learning techniques, as applied to MethaneSAT and MethaneAIR imaging spectroscopic data at varying spatial resolutions, demonstrates the strengths and effectiveness of advanced deep learning architectures in providing robust, scalable solutions for clouds and cloud shadow screening toward enhancing methane emission quantification capacity of existing and next-generation hyperspectral missions. Our data and code are publicly available at: https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/IKLZOJ

title: Rethinking the Flow: A Canal-Based Machine Learning Approach to Urban Flood Detection
content: Machine learning (ML) methods have proven effective for mapping urban flood extents using remotely sensed data. However, the availability of suitable datasets remains a key challenge when training such models. Here, we present a novel approach to address this limitation. Instead of focusing on individual pixels, we use streets as the primary unit of analysis. This change allows us to use an entirely new type of ground-truth dataset: canal cities such as Venice, Italy. Here, we construct a reliable training dataset by labeling canals as flooded streets and regular streets as dry. We then use Sentinel-2 optical remote sensing data to develop an improved neural network architecture specifically designed to accurately classify canals and streets in complex urban environments: the custom street flood index (CSFI). Several other methods are evaluated, ranging from a simple normalized difference water index (NDWI) thresholding approach to more advanced ML techniques such as XGBoost, support vector machine (SVM), random forest, and neural networks. All advanced models yield promising results, significantly outperforming the classic NDWI baseline. However, among them, our new CSFI achieves the best performance, with an accuracy of 86% and a Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) of 72% on our new training dataset. It also shows a strong ability to generalize to previously unseen cities. Finally, the new CFSI is tested on the real-world flood event of Porto Alegre, Brazil, in early May 2024, successfully mapping the extent of the urban flooding with 90% accuracy and an MCC of 70%. This makes our CSFI a new, efficient way to map flood extent at a street scale. The code is made available for users to apply the method to their own urban flood mapping needs.

title: Spatial–Channel Selective State Space Models and Pruned Cross-Task Feature Transfer for Building Height Mapping From Aerial Images and DSMs
content: Building height mapping is widely coupled with footprint segmentation in a unified multitask learning (MTL) framework. In this study, we comprehensively explore the designs of the shared encoder, the bottleneck, and the decoders’ cross-task feature sharing in MTL for precise building height mapping, and propose Mamba-based building height estimation network (MBHNet). First, we discovered that Mamba, a state space model (SSM) variant with selective feature extraction capacity, is particularly suited for inferring building heights. However, in previous remote sensing (RS) related studies, only the global feature correlation capacity, the same as Transformer, is utilized. We design a local-then-global feature encoder, locally sensitive SSM (LS-SSM), which integrates neighborhood attention (NATTEN), input discriminant enhancer (IDE), and spatial-dimension selective SSM, ensuring rich local-to-global information extraction and strong adaptability for MTL tasks. Second, a channel-dimension selective SSM (CSSM)-based bottleneck is introduced to model interchannel dependencies, in which, unlike previous works, the channel-wise information is not compressed and fully preserved. Third, considering the redundancy of the commonly used full feature sharing strategy across different tasks, we develop a K-top relevant feature mining (KTM) strategy in the decoding stage to enable selective and pruned cross-task feature transfer. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that MBHNet consistently outperforms recent methods, achieving very significant relative gains of 12.7%, 32.7%/40.2%, and 32.8% in mean absolute error (MAE) for building height estimation on the GeoNRW dataset, Tainjin Test Set-I/II, and the ISPRS Potsdam dataset, respectively. The datasets used in this study will be made publicly available at: https://github.com/yudawen/MBHNet

title: AIG2AN: Ambiguous-Interpretation Generalized GAN for Self-Supervised Raster-Vector Semantic Segmentation for Cross-Modal Remote Sensing Image
content: Remote sensing image interpretation faces easily overlooked issues: 1) ambiguity: mutual occlusion between objects, e.g., buildings are occluded by trees and vice versa, which leads to ambiguity (building or tree) in GIS, and 2) expression conflicts: topology conflicts between the occluding and occluded objects. However, there have been no reports on the remote sensing ambiguous interpretation (RSAI) or its dataset (RSAID). To address these issues, an RSAID was built, and an ambiguous-interpretation generalized generative adversarial network (AIG2AN) with self-supervised raster-vector semantic segmentation for remote sensing images is proposed for the first time. With the occluding image generated, AIG2AN not only recognizes occlusion regarded as cheating but also restores the occluded semantic labels, which includes three modules: 1) random occlusion generator automatically generates the occluding image with the occluding and occluded labels for self-supervised learning with avoiding hallucination issue; 2) ambiguity dual-interpreters, including both occluding and occluded interpretations, is the first to be proposed; and 3) consistency contrastive learning module (C2LM) is to process the inconsistent interpretations in unambiguous region. Furthermore, we propose a novel multispectral cross-modal learning paradigm via self-supervised pseudo-labeling to automatically enhance the generalization of our AIG2AN in different sensor modalities, and propose a conflict-free and regularized raster-vector expression for interpretation results of ambiguous objects. Experiments on our manually restored RSAID from ISPRS Vaihingen and Potsdam show that AIG2AN improves mIoU and building IoU by 3.28% and 1.80%, with an ambiguity elimination index (AEI) of 85.32% and 76.41%, respectively. Moreover, we reveal the occlusion restoration mechanism. RSAID and code are available at: https://github.com/wenlailiu/AIG2AN

title: Moving Infrared Small Target Detection via Motion-Aided Integrated Spatial–Temporal Network
content: Moving infrared small target detection (MIRSTD) is crucial for various civilian applications. Motion information plays a key role in detecting and locating moving targets in complex infrared scenarios. Existing methods have primarily focused on designing and optimizing robust, complex 3-D spatial–temporal modules to process multiple consecutive input frames to extract motion information, yet they have largely overlooked the description of motion and optimization of input strategies, which limits their efficiency. In this work, we propose a novel motion-aided spatial–temporal integrated network (MIST-Net). We first optimize the input strategy by encoding the dominant motion characteristics across multiple consecutive frames using series expansion and temporal motion intensity analysis, thereby constructing a motion frame that consolidates motion information from several frames into one. The motion frame and target frame are then fed into the integrated network for extracting motion and spatial features, respectively. Additionally, to account for the mutual constraints and dependencies between motion and spatial information, we introduce a mutual calibration module (MCM) that refines the motion maps using spatial cues and enhances the spatial maps with motion information. To further improve the robustness of MIST-Net, we address the semantic gap between motion and spatial features by presenting a motion-spatial-coupled selective state space module (MSCM). Specifically, we generate a motion-spatial coupled state based on the mutual dependencies between the motion hidden state and spatial motion state using entropy theory. This coupled state dynamically adjusts the fusion of motion and spatial information. We conduct extensive experiments on the public SIRST and ITSDT-15K datasets, and the results demonstrate that MIST-Net outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in both accuracy and speed.

title: Ivan-ISTD: Rethinking Cross-Domain Heteroscedastic Noise Perturbations in Infrared Small Target Detection
content: Infrared small target detection (ISTD) plays an important role in drone-based multimodality sensing. To address the dual challenges of cross-domain shift and heteroscedastic noise perturbations in ISTD, we propose a doubly wavelet-guided invariance learning framework (Ivan-ISTD). In the first stage, we generate training samples aligned with the target domain using wavelet-guided cross-domain synthesis. This wavelet-guided alignment machine accurately separates the target background through multifrequency wavelet filtering. In the second stage, we introduce real-domain noise invariance learning, which extracts real noise characteristics from the target domain to build a dynamic noise library. The model learns noise invariance through self-supervised loss, thereby overcoming the limitations of distribution bias in traditional artificial noise modeling. Finally, we create the dynamic-ISTD benchmark, a cross-domain dynamic degradation dataset that simulates the distribution shifts encountered in real-world applications. In addition, we validate the versatility of our method using other real-world datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of many quantitative metrics. In particular, Ivan-ISTD demonstrates excellent robustness in cross-domain scenarios. The code for this work can be found at: https://github.com/nanjin1/Ivan-ISTD

title: High-Fidelity GPR Diffraction Extraction via PSO-Guided Reflection Reconstruction in Wavelet-Dip Domains
content: Ground-penetrating radar (GPR) diffractions typically manifest weak responses resulting from electromagnetic wave interactions with subwavelength heterogeneities (e.g., cracks and voids) or geometric discontinuities (e.g., scattering edges and corners). These subtle yet informative wavefield components are critical for enabling enhanced illumination and dedicated imaging of subsurface microfeatures in infrastructure assessment and geological hazard mitigation. Current diffraction extraction methods in GPR face significant practical limitations: kinematic local-slope techniques are computationally intensive and may lead to loss of vertex events, whereas coherence analysis (CA) requires meticulous calibration of scaling factors and time-moveout corrections. Such constraints can compromise the resolution of imaging modalities, such as migration, tomography, and full-waveform inversion (FWI). To address these challenges, this study introduces an adaptive diffraction extraction workflow that integrates three core modules in wavelet and dip domains: a wavelet-based reflection model for structural preservation, dip-adaptive smoothing operators for wavefield decomposition, and particle swarm optimization (PSO) with multiobjective criteria (Laplacian sharpness, wavelet entropy (WE), and focusing metrics) for parameters optimization. The workflow iteratively extracts residual diffractions by optimizing wavelet decomposition levels, smoothing scales, and residual thresholds using PSO-driven fitness functions. Validated through the heterogeneous random media model, the Sigsbee2a benchmark, and field experiments, the PSO-guided workflow demonstrates quantifiable improvements in edge preservation, coherence artifacts reduction, spatial resolution, and kinematic consistency. By prioritizing the wavefield integrity and structural fineness, the proposed method provides refined inputs for subsequent imaging, highlighting its potential to advance the resolution and reliability of near-surface GPR sensing applications.

title: Semi-Supervised Learning-Based Permanent Scatterer Selection Method for GB-InSAR
content: Permanent scatterer (PS) selection is a critical step in ground-based interferometric synthetic aperture radar (GB-InSAR) measurements. Conventional methods, often constrained by empirical thresholds, demonstrate limited capability in selecting PSs within low-coherence regions caused by deformations. Deep learning provides a data-driven alternative to characterize PS features. However, PSs lack an absolute truth standard. Current deep learning-based PS selection methods predominantly employ fully supervised learning approaches, where significant label noise exists in the training sets. This interferes with the feature learning process of networks and consequently reduces PS recall rates. To overcome these limitations, this article proposes a semi-supervised learning-based PS selection method for GB-InSAR. By establishing a closed-loop optimization path of “refining the labels of training datasets and adjusting the strategy of model training,” this method suppresses the negative impact of label noise and enables the network to accurately fit the PS features, thus enhancing selection performance. First, initial reliable labels are generated via dual-threshold amplitude dispersion methods. Then, unlabeled pixels are processed through an optimal transport formulation solved via Sinkhorn–Knopp algorithm to improve label completeness. Finally, a mean teacher framework is introduced for semi-supervised training of the PS selection network, to systematically leverage implicit features of unlabeled data. A 3-D U-Net serves as the base network for pseudo-label generation and semi-supervised training, which processes sequential interferograms to output PS selection results. Simulated and measured datasets demonstrate the method’s superiority over fully supervised and conventional methods, which significantly increases PS density and quantity while ensuring quality, and it particularly exhibits superior performance in deformation regions.

title: HGA2-FSL: A Heterogeneous Graph Aware-Aggregation Driven Few-Shot Learning Network for Hyperspectral Image Change Detection
content: Hyperspectral image (HSI) provides reliable data support for repeated observations of the overlap area through fine spectral reflectance data from numerous bands, enabling accurate capture of regional variation characteristics. However, the vast spectral and spatial information in HSI makes manual annotation laborious and complex. Practical applications thus demand models that achieve high performance with minimal labeled training data. Pay attention to this practical need, we propose a heterogeneous graph aware-aggregation driven few-shot learning (FSL) network (HGA2-FSL). This network enhances model representation through flexible graph construction and aims to improve the accuracy of hyperspectral change detection (CD) under limited labeling by transferring change priors learned from other images. Specifically, we first design a correlation aggregation module (CAM) to build multilevel graph structures that incorporate various types of geographic feature relationships, promoting effective capture and aware-aggregation of local and long-range information. In addition, through joint FSL on very-high-resolution image (VHRI) and HSI, the method utilizes the abundant labeled samples from VHRI and transfers learnable information, reducing the need for labeled samples of HSI. Meanwhile, we design a cross-domain node update-based domain adaptation (DA) method to explore a subspace that minimizes distribution differences, thereby reducing the modality gap between VHRI and HSI and improving the transfer learning performance. Extensive experiments on three public HSI-CD datasets validate the superior performance of HGA2-FSL. The code will be made publicly available at: https://github.com/lsylnnu/HGA2-FSL

title: Grouped Attention-Based Spectral–Spatial Feature Learning for Collaborative Hyperspectral and LiDAR Classification
content: The attention mechanism, through its dynamic weight allocation and feature enhancement, effectively improves the representation capability of land-cover features in hyperspectral imaging and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data. However, most implementations of attention mechanisms tend to neglect the local attributes of land-cover features and the prior information inherent in hyperspectral imagery (HSI) data. Grouped feature learning mechanisms can focus on different characteristics or structures of land-cover objects. Based on this, this article integrates a grouping mechanism into the convolutional neural networks (CNNs)-Transformer framework and proposes a grouped attention-based spectral–spatial feature learning (GAS2-FL) network. Exploiting the low-rank prior of HSI data, the network incorporates a spectral–spatial feature learning module and embeds a grouped feature learning strategy to model spectral information interactions among heterogeneous features. Furthermore, a grouped token mechanism and a nonlocal token learning mechanism are developed to enhance the interaction of local attribute features and the learning of fused representations. The superior performance of the proposed network on four HSI and LiDAR classification benchmarks comprehensively demonstrates that its grouped feature learning mechanism is highly effective at extracting and representing the discriminative features essential for land-cover characterization. The source codes will be available at https://github.com/RSIP-NJUPT/GAS2-FL

title: DF-PEM: Dual-Flow Prompt-Expert Mamba for Multimodal Remote Sensing Incremental Classification
content: Existing deep learning-based multimodal remote sensing imagery (MRSI) classification (MRSIC) models rely on fixed-category paradigms and struggle to adapt to novel categories, primarily due to the catastrophic forgetting of vintage knowledge, which substantially impedes their practical applications. To overcome this challenge, we propose a novel dual-flow prompt-expert Mamba (DF-PEM) network for MRSI incremental classification (IC), which incorporates dedicated parallel branches for spectral and spatial feature extraction, synergized with a cross-modal fusion module to integrate modality-specific representations. The primary objective of DF-PEM is to capture discriminative MRSI features while preserving knowledge of previously seen categories during the incremental learning process. Specifically, DF-PEM integrates the prompt-driven assistant-expert (PAE) mechanism within the Mamba architecture to dynamically activate category-specific prompt experts per input, enabling seamless adaptation to emerging categories while mitigating knowledge erosion of prior classes. Furthermore, a spherical Gaussian sift (SGS) strategy is developed in the initial stage, which models the complex feature distributions of historical categories and neutralizes noise and outlier effects, yielding robust representative prototypes. Finally, a prototype-aware regularization classifier (PRC) that adaptively balances prediction bias between incremental and old categories by learning regularization coefficients, which strategically penalize parameter updates that deviate the model from historical knowledge, further enhancing knowledge retention and generalization. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed DF-PEM significantly outperforms existing methods, validating its effectiveness for MRSI classification tasks.

title: Polarization Super-Resolution Remote Sensing Imaging Based on Compressed Sensing and Deep Learning
content: Remote sensing is widely applied in environmental monitoring, Earth observation, and military reconnaissance, with increasing demands for higher spatial resolution, improved contrast, and multidimensional feature acquisition. Polarization imaging, particularly enabled by division-of-focal-plane (DoFP) polarization detectors, offers advantages in material differentiation and structural enhancement due to its ability to capture intensity, angle, and degree of polarization (DoP) simultaneously in a compact, snapshot configuration. However, current research and applications of DoFP-based polarization imaging still encounter several key challenges: first, spatial resolution degradation and interchannel aliasing arising from pixel-level downsampling and field-of-view (FoV) misalignment; second, the absence of physical priors in most deep learning models, which limits their interpretability and robustness; finally, maintaining image quality under sparse sampling conditions remains a significant technical challenge. To overcome these challenges, we propose polarization compressed sensing generative adversarial network (PCSGAN), a dual-stage polarization super-resolution framework that couples compressed sensing theory with deep generative modeling, enhanced by polarization degradation simulation and sparse regularization. The original DoFP imaging resolution itself is half that of the detector’s pixel scale. In contrast, the proposed method achieves  $4\times $  super-resolution reconstruction with only 6.25% sampling (equivalent to a single snapshot), effectively doubling the native spatial resolution of the detector while achieving high-fidelity polarization information recovery. We validate our approach on three public datasets and one self-collected dataset. Compared with six state-of-the-art methods across 20 types of remote sensing scenes, PCSGAN consistently achieves the best average results, improving peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) by 2.3%–11.7% and structural similarity index (SSIM) by 0.1%–12.8%. The effectiveness of PCSGAN is further demonstrated through uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV)-based remote sensing experiments, showing strong potential for real-time, high-precision polarization imaging applications.

title: Evaluation and Verification of Different Characterization Methods of Echo Signal for the AEMS/ACDL
content: In view of the strong subjectivity in existing researches regarding the accurate extraction of the echo signal from the aerosol and carbon detection lidar (ACDL) onboard the atmospheric environment monitoring satellite (AEMS), a systematic evaluation of the pulse peak method (PPM), pulse integration method (PIM), and pulse fitting method (PFM) is conducted. Echo signals from four typical geographical regions, namely the plain, mountain, ocean, and plateau, are analyzed to explore the applicability of different signal extraction methods under varying peak intensities and signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs). The differential absorption optical depth (DAOD) calculated by the PIM exhibits higher stability and smaller standard deviation than those obtained using the PPM and PFM, indicating that the PIM is more suitable for most geographical regions. In order to reduce the errors introduced by the uncertainties of meteorological data, a self-verification approach based on the micro-region stability assumption is proposed as the evaluation index of comparing the performance of three signal extraction schemes, providing a practical solution when the external verification data are unavailable. Computer simulations further demonstrate that the number of integration points for achieving the maximum SNR is inversely proportional to the mean SNR of the signal, offering a quantitative guidance for enhancing echo signal quality through appropriate integration points selection. The inversion accuracy of the XCO2 obtained using different signal extraction methods is evaluated, and the PIM achieves the smallest XCO2 deviation, with an average deviation of −0.43 ppm compared with the XCO2 data observed by the global total carbon column observing Network (TCCON) at the Xianghe Station. This study provides a practical, efficient, and robust technical framework for the XCO2 inversion of the new generation lidar satellite AEMS together with offering the valuable technical support for the global CO2 monitoring applications.

title: Unlocking Shallow Subsurface Architecture in Coastal Zones: Integrated ESPAC-Genetic Algorithm Inversion With Sparse Drilling Constraints
content: High-resolution shear-wave velocity (Vs) characterization is essential for mitigating seismic hazards and enhancing infrastructure resilience in coastal megacities; however, conventional active-source methods often prove impractical in soft-sediment, infrastructure-dense environments. This article presents a borehole-constrained extended spatial autocorrelation-genetic algorithm (ESPAC-GA) workflow that integrates sparse microtremor arrays with minimal drilling investment to achieve robust 2-D Vs imaging to 50 m in depth. On the western Pacific coastal plain, a ten-node nested triangular array (58-m aperture) was deployed along a 560-m transect, constrained by three 60-m boreholes serving as constraints or for validation. Hyperparameters (population: 20 and generations: 40) were optimized through 50 Monte Carlo runs, and ensemble averaging of five independent inversions effectively suppressed nonuniqueness. Numerical validation demonstrates layer thickness and Vs errors of approximately 3%, whereas field application—characterized by highly attenuative, water-saturated clays—reveals a characteristic “shallow-sensitive, deep-robust” response. The resulting 2-D Vs profile clearly delineates the marine–lacustrine–tuff sequence and laterally variable bedrock interfaces, with cumulative bedrock depth errors remaining below 6% and bedrock velocity errors of merely −6.9%. This accuracy meets offshore wind foundation and immersed-tunnel engineering specifications while reducing drilling costs by over 30%. Furthermore, the optimized hyperparameters may serve as a useful initial model for analogous deltaic settings worldwide. This optimized workflow offers a cost-effective, scalable tool for real-time, high-resolution Vs mapping in coastal zones, supporting climate-adaptive urban planning and infrastructure risk assessment.

title: Viscoacoustic Least-Squares Reverse Time Migration Using Structure-Oriented Total Variation Norm Regularization
content: Viscoacoustic least-squares reverse time migration (QLSRTM) has emerged as a cutting-edge imaging tool, which can overcome the intrinsic attenuation effects of subsurface media and provide a robust solution for reconstructing the true reflectivity of subsurface. However, the migrated image of QLSRTM generally have side-effects mainly caused by the inaccurate background velocity model, incomplete acquisition geometry, inadequate physical modeling assumptions, and so on. Due to the excellent edge-preserving denoising capability, the total variation (TV) norm regularization has been introduced into the least-squares inversion framework to suppress the side-effects and give a segmented smooth solution. Nevertheless, conventional TV norm constraint solely considers the gradient variation of inversion parameter along the horizontal and vertical directions, which might reduce its adaptability for complex structures. For this reason, we propose a QLSRTM algorithm that integrates a structure-oriented TV norm regularization into the objective function. Specifically, the decoupled fractional Laplacians (DFLs) viscoacoustic wave equation is utilized to accurately characterize the propagation effects of seismic waves in subsurface media. Besides the structural directional information are extracted from the inverted image through gradient structure tensor (GST) method within each iteration and then incorporated into the conventional TV norm to constraint inversion. In this article, we adopted the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMMs) to iteratively solve the constraint structure-oriented TV norm minimization problem. The feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed approach are validated through two synthetic and a field data. Migration results demonstrate that the proposed approach can not only compensate for attenuation effects effectively and minimize the migration artifacts significantly, but also obtain a better quality for imaging complex structures than conventional regularization.

title: Magnetic Target Boundary Identification Using L1 Norm Regularized Inversion and Optimal Slice Selection
content: The identification of magnetic target boundaries from measured field data is a key research area in geophysics. However, existing methods based on magnetic field components or gradient tensors exhibit limitations in their identification effectiveness. To address this deficiency, this study proposes an L1 norm regularized inversion method based on the magnetic moment gradient. Specifically, the method projects the 3-D target onto a 2-D plane and formulates an objective function that leverages the magnetic moment gradient with L1 norm regularization. It then introduces a novel section selection method, which combines a coarse-to-fine strategy with an analysis of fitting term residual variations. This method first generates candidate sections via the coarse-to-fine strategy and then identifies a turning point from the differences in the fitting term residuals across the sections. This point signifies the inversion’s transition from rapid change to stable convergence, thereby determining a robust inversion section. Furthermore, a benchmark-adaptive method for re-weighting the regularization parameter is introduced, which obviates the need for its independent calculation for each section. We further propose rank-based normalization of the inversion results to balance the responses from both strong and weak anomalies. Results from both simulations and field experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves better performance in boundary identification than existing methods.

title: DASODNet: Diffusion Model Assisted Small Object Detection for Remote Sensing lmages
content: Small object detection in remote sensing images is critically significant in both military and civilian fields. Super resolution techniques enhance image details to facilitate more accurate object detection. Diffusion models achieve fine-grained control over image content and stable training through a iterative denoising process during super resolution. However, current diffusion model assisted object detection methods still suffer from issues such as long training times, structural complexity, and insufficient focus on target region. To address these challenges, we propose an end-to-end diffusion model assisted small object detection method. During training, features extracted by the backbone network from low resolution images are fed simultaneously into both the object detection branch and the super-resolution branch for collaborative optimization. This design aligns the regions of interest between the super resolution and object detection tasks, guiding the backbone network to generate high resolution details for small objects. During inference, the super-resolution branch can be completely removed, significantly improving detection speed. To further optimize the architecture, we design a dense condition module that directly generates high resolution conditional features by fusing multiscale features from the backbone network, simplifying the inference pipeline. In addition, we introduce an object-aware super-resolution loss function that focuses on constraining the reconstruction quality of target regions, effectively enhancing the detail representation of small objects while suppressing interference from background information. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms existing methods, improving detection accuracy while maintaining detection speed.

title: Unifying Multiscale Representations in Remote Sensing Image Color Correction With a Mamba-Conv Fusion Network
content: Optical remote sensing images (RSIs) often suffer from color distortions caused by atmospheric scattering, sensor characteristics, and illumination variations, which degrade visual quality and compromise downstream performance. Nondeep learning methods rely on empirical models and artificial parameters, and are difficult to adapt to complex and changeable remote sensing imaging conditions. Existing CNN-based methods are limited by their local receptive fields, while Transformer-based models incur high computational costs when modeling large-scale RSIs. To address these issues, a Mamba-Conv hybrid multiscale fusion network for RSI color correction is proposed in this work. The network leverages an alternating coupling mechanism between convolution and state-space modeling to unify local detail preservation and global illumination consistency, while a multiscale adaptive fusion strategy ensures coherent cross-scale feature alignment. Extensive experiments show that proposed method consistently surpasses current advanced techniques in both quantitative metrics and visual assessments, offering an effective method to alleviate color distortion in remote sensing image and enhance its interpretability and application value.

title: A Method for Generating Large-Scale Spaceborne Long-Wave Infrared Point-Target Images Using Landsat 8 TIRS Data
content: Deep learning has demonstrated remarkable potential in spaceborne infrared small target detection; however, its application is severely bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-quality, annotated on-orbit data. To bridge this “Sim-to-Real” domain gap, this article proposes a radiometrically consistent image generation framework capable of synthesizing large-scale, high-fidelity long-wave infrared (LWIR) datasets. Unlike traditional methods that rely on simplistic image injection or computationally expensive ray-tracing, our approach adopts a hybrid strategy: it leverages the global archive of calibrated Landsat-8 data to ensure authentic background diversity and couples it with physically modeled targets derived from computational fluid dynamics. A key innovation is the radiance-domain fusion mechanism, which integrates targets and backgrounds strictly in physical units, considering atmospheric transmission and sensor degradation effects (PSF, motion blur, noise equivalent delta temperature), thereby guaranteeing energy conservation for subpixel targets. Quantitative validation against real on-orbit imagery from the SDG satellite demonstrates that the proposed framework achieves superior fidelity, with intensity profiles and statistical metrics (HP-PSNR) closely matching real observations. This work provides a scalable solution to the data scarcity problem, offering a valuable foundation for training and benchmarking robust deep learning models in spaceborne surveillance tasks.

title: Quantitative Retrieval of Water-in-Oil Emulsions Thickness Using Airborne Hyperspectral Remote Sensing: A Ground-Based Oil Spill Experiment
content: Under the influence of marine environmental dynamics, oil slicks on the sea surface undergo emulsification, forming various types of oil–water emulsions, including water-in-oil (WO) and oil-in-water (OW) emulsions. The impact of oil spill emulsions on the environment is difficult to eliminate for a long time, so it is of great significance to monitor them. At present, there are few studies on detecting WO emulsions of different thicknesses using hyperspectral remote sensing. In this article, through designing small-scene outdoor experiments for optical remote sensing detection of WO emulsions, analytical spectral devices data and airborne hyperspectral data of WO emulsions with different thicknesses at different times were obtained. The spectral characteristics of WO emulsions with different thicknesses were analyzed, and a quantitative retrieval model for WO emulsion thickness based on a deep belief network (DBN) was constructed. Meanwhile, 24-h thermal infrared data of WO emulsions with different thicknesses were collected. The brightness temperature characteristics of WO emulsions with different thicknesses were analyzed, and the detection capability of WO emulsions with different thicknesses using airborne hyperspectral and thermal infrared remote sensing was evaluated. The experimental results show the following. First, compared with the support vector regression, extreme learning machine, random forest, convolutional neural network, and transformer models, the developed DBN model achieves the optimal retrieval effect for the thickness of WO emulsions, with a root-mean-square error of only 0.054 mm. Second, the accuracy of the combined retrieval of WO emulsions thickness using hyperspectral and thermal infrared is better than that of the single thermal infrared, but lower than that of the hyperspectral remote sensing.

title: MambaSTF: Visual State Space Model for Remote Sensing Images Spatiotemporal Fusion
content: Spatiotemporal fusion (STF) methods are pivotal for generating satellite imagery with high spatial and temporal resolution, enabling continuous, gap-free monitoring of land surface dynamics. While deep learning-based STF has shown remarkable potential, it often struggles to balance computational efficiency with effective long-range dependency modeling and adaptive feature extraction across heterogeneous landscapes. To address these issues, we introduce MambaSTF, a novel STF framework based on visual state space models, capable of capturing long-range contexts with linear complexity. Its core includes a Quad-directional 2D-Selective-Scan (QD-SS2D) mechanism that systematically models anisotropic spatial structures via multidirectional scanning. The dedicated State Space Fusion (SSF) module employs dual orthogonal streams: Spatial Selective-Scan Fusion (S-SSF) interleaves multiresolution features to enhance context, while Temporal Selective-Scan Fusion (T-SSF) aligns multitemporal sequences to capture dynamics. These are adaptively fused through a gating mechanism, enabling context-aware spatiotemporal integration. Extensive experiments across diverse datasets confirm its superiority in reconstructing complex landscapes under varying atmospheric conditions. Rigorous uncertainty quantification further demonstrates operational stability and robustness, even amid abrupt land cover changes.

title: Automatic Water-Land Segmentation Algorithm via Weighted Mixture Model With Dual Spatial Constraints
content: Water-land segmentation faces significant challenges due to land-water interface heterogeneity. Existing methods based on spatially constrained statistical models often yield unsatisfactory segmentation results. To address this limitation, this article proposes an automatic water-land segmentation algorithm based on a weighted Gaussian mixture model with dual spatial constraints (DSC-WGMM). First, a weighted Gaussian mixture model (WGMM) is established to characterize the complex statistical distributions of heterogeneous regions. The dual weights of the WGMM effectively leverage pixel spatial correlations. Next, Markov random fields (MRF) are constructed on both subclass and class weights to introduce dual spatial constraints into the WGMM, thereby further reducing the influence of heterogeneous pixels. Finally, model parameters are estimated using the expectation-maximization (EM) method, while the optimal smoothing coefficient is determined through a gradient optimization method. This combination enables fast and adaptive water-land segmentation. Numerous experiments on high-resolution remote sensing images demonstrate that the proposed algorithm accurately fits complex histograms, reduces heterogeneous pixel interference, and achieves precise water-land segmentation. The proposed algorithm consistently outperforms comparative methods. It demonstrates a significant improvement in accuracy and adaptivity of water-land segmentation, offering an effective and robust technical method for waterline extraction in complex scenes.

title: AEZGO: A Novel Approach for Mapping Mangrove Canopy Height Using UAV Lidar Data and Sentinel-2 Images
content: Accurate mangrove canopy height mapping is essential for assessing blue carbon and protecting coastal communities. Yet, existing methods face dual challenges: 1) spectral saturation causing systematic bias (underestimation of tall trees and overestimation of short trees), and 2) spatial heterogeneity of mangrove ecosystems impairing model generalizability across large areas. This study develops an automatic ecological zoning and geo-balanced optimization approach integrating Sentinel-2 imagery, UAV lidar data, and mangrove ecological knowledge to address the challenges. The approach involves: 1) automatic ecological zoning via an adaptive weighted partitioning algorithm based on four ecological knowledge types (environmental factors, spectral characteristics, canopy height–spectral correlations, and administrative boundaries) to reduce spatial heterogeneity, and 2) geo-balanced optimization integrates sample balancing, feature selection, and spatial covariates to resolve spectral saturation in spectral–height relationships. Applied to Hainan Island, China, the approach achieved significantly higher accuracy (R2 = 0.74, RMSE = 1.12 m) compared to traditional random forest (R2 = 0.59, RMSE = 1.34 m). Notably, it reduced balanced mean errors (aME) by 42% for tall trees (>7 m, 2.57 to 1.49 m) and 37% for short trees (<3 m, –0.76 to –0.48 m). The resultant mangrove canopy height map revealed an average height of 3.77 m across Hainan’s mangroves. Compared to the existing mangrove canopy height maps from other studies, our approach yielded the most accurate results for Hainan Island. The proposed approach shows potential for broader application, thereby supporting large-scale precision management of mangrove-related tasks such as carbon sink accounting and the evaluation of coastal protection services like windbreak and wave dissipation.

title: IVD-NET: An Adaptive Dual-Branch Network for Small Object Detection in Multimodal Remote Sensing Images
content: To address challenges such as drastic illumination changes, dense small objects, and frequent occlusions in remote sensing and UAV Images, this article proposes an infrared-visible dual-branch detection network (IVD-NET). Existing fusion methods often overlook cross-modal feature alignment and insufficient representation of small objects. To tackle these issues, IVD-NET employs a dual-stream architecture with three key innovations. First, an adaptive modality fusion module comprising cross-modal correlation attention and cross-modal guided pixel attention is proposed to dynamically integrate complementary information from infrared and visible modalities, effectively handling lighting interference. Second, a visual parallel aggregation module is designed to enhance feature representation for small objects through parallel multi-scale branches and frequency-domain attention. Finally, a dual-path adaptive detection head integrating the spatial enhanced attention module is introduced to improve localization and classification accuracy for occluded targets. Extensive experiments on DroneVehicle and LLVIP datasets demonstrate that IVD-NET achieves state-of-the-art performance. Specifically, on the DroneVehicle dataset, it achieves an mAP50 of 83.08%, outperforming methods like YOLOv10 and MMDetection.

title: Quantitative Interpretation of the Penetration Process of L-Band SAR Signals in Mapping Chinese Fir Forest Canopy Heights With Quad-Polarization SAOCOM-1 Images
content: The penetrating characteristics of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) signals are widely recognized as a key advantage over optical remote sensing data. However, this penetration capability can lead to an underestimation of forest canopy height when interferometric SAR (InSAR) or polarimetric interferometric SAR techniques are used, particularly with L-band SAR signals. Due to the complex internal structure of forests and the strong penetration ability of L-band SAR signals, quantitatively characterizing how these signals interact with forest canopies remains a significant challenge. To address the limitations imposed by SAR signal penetration on mapping forest canopy height, simulated L-band quad-polarization data and spaceborne SAOCOM-1 images were utilized to analyze the interaction mechanisms between L-band SAR signals and forest canopies with InSAR technology. Subsequently, two quantitative parameters, penetration depth height (PDH) and penetration depth rate (PDR), were introduced to measure penetration depth and examine its variation across different forest types and polarization channels. Finally, by evaluating the relationships between these proposed parameters and actual forest heights, a novel forest height mapping approach was proposed by integrating the XGBoost model with PDH-related variables derived from SAOCOM-1 imagery, with the assistance of GF-7 stereo images. The results indicated that the penetration depth of L-band SAR signals in forests is strongly influenced by tree species, forest height, and canopy density. Coniferous forests exhibited significantly greater penetration capability compared to broad-leaved forests. Moreover, the PDH across the three polarization channels followed a consistent order from shallow to deep: HV, VV, and HH. With increasing forest height and density, the PDR in coniferous forests gradually decreased to 0.5, corresponding to the lower canopy layers, whereas in broad-leaved forests, the penetration depth was predominantly observed in the upper half of the canopy. Among the quantified parameters, PDH demonstrated the strongest correlation with forest height. The results also demonstrated that the proposed method for forest height mapping yielded promising performance, achieving root-mean-square error (RMSE) values of 3.86 and 5.01 m, with corresponding relative RMSE (rRMSE) values of 21.7% and 36.2% for coniferous and broad-leaved forests, respectively. These findings suggest that the proposed approach has considerable potential for large-area forest height mapping.

title: MTCS: A Multitask Framework for End-to-End UAV-Based Photovoltaic Panels Damage Assessment
content: Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) provide an extremely effective solution for environmental monitoring and disaster assessment. Counting tasks are widely applied in UAV-based remote sensing to accurately estimate the number and distribution of objects, particularly in applications such as disaster damage assessment. However, relying solely on counting results limits detailed statistical analysis of specific regions, thereby restricting the potential of downstream tasks. This article proposes a multitask counting and segmentation (MTCS) model that enables concurrent segmentation and counting in a single forward pass, specifically applied to UAV damage assessment. The backbone network, termed the task-agnostic feature network (TAFNet), utilizes nonlearnable primitives (NLPs) to mitigate conflicts between tasks. In addition, we introduce the frequency fusion module (FFM) to replace conventional concatenation methods, thereby enhancing the differentiation of boundary features. We also propose the CBM and SBM to enhance the performance of counting and segmentation tasks, respectively. To enable end-to-end analysis, we introduce the area-specific analysis method (ASAM). Furthermore, we construct a UAV-based remote sensing multitask dataset, PV-CountSeg, which contains data on photovoltaic panels damaged by hail. Extensive experiments conducted on the PV-CountSeg, CARPK, and iSAID datasets demonstrate that MTCS achieves competitive accuracy in counting and segmentation compared to existing single-task models. MTCS is the first multitask model specifically designed for joint segmentation and counting, offering a unified paradigm for downstream tasks in remote sensing regional statistics.

title: GASE-Net: Spatial Channel Enhancement and Adaptive Fusion of Graph Attention for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: In recent years, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and graph neural networks (GNNs) have been widely applied to hyperspectral remote sensing image classification. However, CNNs rely on fixed-size convolutional kernels for feature extraction, resulting in limited receptive fields that struggle to capture relationships between distant pixels in hyperspectral images. While GNN-based hyperspectral classification methods effectively mine features in non-Euclidean hyperspectral data, they face significant challenges in constructing graph structures. For instance, traditional graph convolutional networks typically rely on local neighborhood connections, making it difficult to directly model spatial relationships between pixels at extreme distances. This limits their ability to effectively extract long-range interaction information within images. To address these challenges, this article proposes GASE-Net, a network for hyperspectral image classification. Adopting a parallel architecture, it integrates a CNN with channel–spatial attention and a gated graph attention network, while incorporating noise reduction and feature enhancement modules at the front end to boost feature representation capabilities. The proposed network fuses features from two branches through an adaptive weighting mechanism. On the one hand, it models and captures spectral–spatial features from non-Euclidean hyperspectral images based on graph structures, effectively characterizing relationships between pixels at ultra-long distances. On the other hand, it employs a pixel-level CNN with a fused channel–spatial attention mechanism to extract fine-grained spatial features, thereby overcoming the inherent limitations of CNNs in modeling long-range relationships. Experiments on the Salina, WHU-Hi-HanChuan, WHU-Hi-HongHu, Pingan, Qingyun, and Indian Pines dataset, the proposed method achieves overall classification accuracies of 99.33%, 98.24%, 98.57%, 98.41%, 97.83%, and 94.02%, respectively. Compared with other models, GASE-Net demonstrates strong generalization capabilities and excellent classification performance in hyperspectral remote sensing image classification.

title: A Parameter-Adaptive Photon Denoising Algorithm for ICESat-2 Glacier Monitoring in Tibet
content: Accurate extraction of glacier surface elevation from ice, cloud, and land elevation satellite 2 (ICESat-2) photon-counting data is fundamental for monitoring glacial morphology and assessing ice mass balance. However, effectively suppressing noise while retaining signal photons remains a significant challenge in complex alpine environments. To address this issue, we propose a parameter-adaptive photon denoising (PAPD) algorithm for mountain glacier monitoring. The method incorporates an optimized coarse-denoising stage to minimize the risk of erroneous signal rejection and introduces an innovative adaptive elliptical neighborhood mechanism that dynami cally adjusts based on local photon distributions, thereby enhancing adaptability to terrain variations. We evaluated the algorithm using four ICESat-2 datasets with varying signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) across the southeastern Tibetan Plateau. The experimental results based on the reference dataset indicate that PAPD achieves a superior average F1-score of 0.980, effectively outperforming benchmark algorithms, including DBSCAN (0.966), RANSAC (0.907), ATL03 (0.883), and adaptive DBSCAN (0.836). Notably, in regions with low SNR or abrupt topographic changes, the proposed method yields elevation profiles with improved continuity and richer local details. Validation against ATL06 elevation products further corroborates its robustness. These findings suggest that PAPD provides a reliable data foundation for high-resolution glacier elevation series, demonstrating significant potential for precise mass balance estimation in challenging alpine glacier environments.

title: Category-Aware Asymmetric Dynamic Fusion Network for Multimodal Semantic Segmentation of Remote Sensing Images
content: Most existing multimodal fusion methods adopt a symmetric fusion strategy to optimize feature consistency between modalities. However, this strategy ignores modality-specific discrepancies, and its equal weight distribution mechanism exacerbates the class imbalance problem, resulting in poor classification performance for small-sample categories, such as “road.” To address these issues, this study proposes a category-aware asymmetric dynamic fusion network (CADFNet), which realizes cross-modal differentiated feature interaction guided by class-level information through the synergy of a multilevel difference pyramid fusion (MDPF) module and a dynamic sparse category attention (DSCA) module. The MDPF module fully mines and enhances cross-modal complementary information by explicitly modeling multiscale differential features to address the problem of insufficient utilization of modal difference features in existing methods; The DSCA module uses a dynamic sparsity mechanism in conjunction with semantic perception to adaptively assign modal weights to different categories, achieving refined alignment and fusion of cross-modal key areas, thereby effectively alleviating the class imbalance problem. Comprehensive experiments on the PIE-RGB-SAR and WHU-OPT-SAR datasets demonstrate that CADFNet achieves excellent performance. In particular, on the WHU-OPT-SAR dataset, its mean intersection over union shows improvements ranging from 1.99% to 15.32% over other state-of-the-art methods.

title: SSBDM: A Spectral–Spatial Bilinear Decomposition Model With Adaptive Multikernel Dictionary for Hyperspectral Target Detection
content: Hyperspectral target detection remains challenging due to complex nonlinear spectral mixing and insufficient incorporation of spatial context. Existing approaches often address these issues in isolation: bilinear models neglect spatial coherence, kernel-based methods require empirical selection of kernel types and parameters, and many spatial-contextual methods lack explicit modeling of material scattering effects. To address these limitations, we propose a spectral–spatial bilinear decomposition model (SSBDM) that explicitly models scattering-induced nonlinearity and exploits spatial-contextual information to enhance target representation under nonlinear mixing. Specifically, a bilinear mixing model (BMM) with second-order interaction terms is adopted to capture scattering effects beyond linear approximations, providing improved physical interpretability. Moreover, we introduce an adaptive multikernel automatic target generation strategy that dynamically fuses multiple kernels based on projection residuals, eliminating manual kernel selection while constructing a high-purity, scene-adaptive background dictionary. Furthermore, we propose dual spectral–spatial regularizations for coefficient estimation, consisting of a superpixel-based graph Laplacian to preserve local spatial homogeneity and mitigate spectral distortion, and a double reweighted sparsity constraint that downweights low-contributing atoms (particularly second-order interaction atoms) to stabilize bilinear coefficient estimation. This dual design simultaneously enforces spatial coherence and selective sparsity, enhancing robustness and target–background separability under nonlinear mixing. Extensive experiments on four real-world datasets validate the effectiveness and robustness of SSBDM under spectral variability and limited supervision, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.

title: Evaluating InSAR Strategies for Deformation Monitoring in Urban Densely Constructed Areas
content: Urban engineering construction can cause ground disturbances and pose potential risks to surrounding infrastructure. As a deformation monitoring technique offering extensive coverage and high data stability, interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR) is well suited for urban safety management applications. These include the back analysis of sudden geological events during construction, large-scale deformation mapping, and geohazard risk assessment. However, the effectiveness of InSAR, which involves diverse data sources and processing methodologies, varies significantly across different scenarios. This article takes a typical densely constructed area in Bao'an District, Shenzhen, as a case study. It systematically evaluates the performance of different data sources and parameter configurations for monitoring deformation in urban densely constructed areas. Based on the findings, recommendations for selecting appropriate InSAR strategies for various practical scenarios are provided.

title: Parallel GLRT-Based SAR Tomographic Processing on Distributed Multi-GPU Platforms
content: This article addresses the development of a multilevel parallel algorithm for detecting single scatterers in SAR tomography, targeted at multinode multi-GPU distributed computational architectures. Taking into account the computational structure of the canonical processing scheme based on the generalized likelihood ratio test considered in this article, an appropriate problem decomposition is adopted to devise and formulate an efficient parallel algorithm tailored for heterogeneous high-performance computing platforms, thereby enabling a hierarchical exploitation of different levels of parallelism, including both distributed and shared memory models. Experimental evaluations conducted on a multinode, GPU-enabled HPC cluster, using a high-resolution SAR dataset, exhibit substantial acceleration and scalability. These results quantitatively confirm the effectiveness of the proposed multilevel parallel framework and position the developed prototype as a promising, scalable solution for large-scale SAR tomographic processing applications. This is particularly relevant in light of upcoming SAR missions, which are expected to generate unprecedented volumes of data, thus demanding scalable and high-performance processing solutions.

title: Center-Guided Dynamic Convolutional Network for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: In hyperspectral image classification, effectively modeling the relationship between a target pixel and its surrounding context remains challenging. While Transformer-based approaches achieve promising results through self-attention mechanisms, their quadratic computational complexity limits practical deployment. In this article, we propose a novel center-guided dynamic convolutional network (CGDC-Net) that enhances convolutional neural network architectures with input-adaptive convolutions and center-guided attention mechanisms. CGDC-Net introduces the central dynamic convolution (CDConv) module that employs dynamically modulated kernels adapting to each input sample based on multiscale contextual information centered around the target pixel. Through efficient tensor decomposition, CDConv generates sample-specific kernel modulations while maintaining computational efficiency. Building upon CDConv, we further incorporate center-guided convolutional block attention that integrates center-guided channel attention and center-guided spatial attention to explicitly emphasize the target pixel while capturing discriminative spectral–spatial features. By combining dynamic convolutions with attention mechanisms, CGDC-Net achieves enhanced feature representation while maintaining CNN's computational efficiency. Extensive experiments on three public HSI datasets demonstrate that CGDC-Net outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

title: ZELPHA: A Zoom-Equivariant Lipschitz Prototype Framework for Remote Sensing Aerial Scene Classification
content: This article proposes zoom-equivariant Lipschitz prototype hypothesis (ZELPHA), a unified framework for robust remote sensing (RS) scene classification under scale variations. The method integrates scale-equivariant representation learning with Lipschitz-constrained stability and prototype-based classification to achieve consistent cross-scale feature representations. Theoretical analysis establishes stability properties, while experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate improved robustness, especially under small-scale conditions. Compared with single-technique approaches, the joint design provides better cross-scale consistency and generalization across varying imaging resolutions. The results indicate that ZELPHA offers an effective and practical solution for handling scale variability in real-world RS applications.

title: Terrain-Aware Optical-SAR Image Registration Under Severe Geometric Distortions
content: Accurate registration between optical and synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery remains challenging, particularly in rugged terrain, where SAR-specific geometric distortions significantly degrade correspondence reliability. Existing optical-SAR registration methods primarily focus on mitigating radiometric discrepancies across sensors, while the terrain-dependent failure mechanisms induced by SAR imaging geometry have received limited attention. In this article, we reformulate optical-SAR image registration as a geometry-consistency correction problem across heterogeneous imaging models, rather than a direct multimodal matching task. A digital surface model (DSM) is introduced as an explicit geometric proxy that bridges optical and SAR projections, transforming geometric distortions from sources of interference into usable constraints for correspondence establishment. Based on this formulation, a terrain-aware two-stage registration framework is developed. In the first stage, reliable correspondences are established in geometry-dominant rugged regions through DSM-mediated projection consistency. In the second stage, these correspondences are propagated to appearance-dominant flat regions using a distortion-aware multimodal matching strategy. A unified block adjustment model integrates correspondences from different terrain regimes to achieve globally consistent coregistration. Extensive experiments on 1108 image pairs and multiple challenging rugged scenarios demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms representative approaches in terms of registration accuracy and robustness, particularly under severe geometric distortions.

title: HDF-Net: A Hybrid Deep Learning Model for Retrieving Ozone Profiles in the Arctic
content: Continuous, high-temporal-resolution vertical distribution data for ozone (O3) in the Arctic is crucial for understanding atmospheric chemistry and the dynamics of this climate-sensitive region. However, obtaining such detailed profiles remains challenging, owing to sparse observations. This study introduces a hybrid deep-learning fusion network model (HDF-Net) as a reanalysis-constrained reconstruction approach to retrieve O3 profiles by synergistically fusing ground-based multiaxis differential optical absorption spectroscopy (MAX-DOAS) observations with meteorological reanalysis data. In the multibranch architecture of HDF-Net, a convolutional neural network extracts vertical gradients features from ERA5 meteorological profiles, while parallel bidirectional long short-term memory networks capture sequential information from MAX-DOAS slant column densities (SCDs) and differential SCDs (DSCDs). A cross-attention module is integrated to dynamically fuse these deep features, enabling retrieval of 25-layer O3 profiles from the near-surface to altitudes of ca. 40 km with minute-level resolution. HDF-Net outperformed traditional machine learning and single-modality deep-learning baselines, particularly in reducing tropospheric errors. Rigorous validation confirmed its robust performance. HDF-Net achieved good agreement with data obtained using ozonesondes (peak R, 0.89) and consistent trends with stratospheric microwave limb sounder data (peak R, 0.84) and traditional total column retrievals (R, 0.94). Additionally, it captured near-surface variability, validated via in situ measurement (R, 0.52). HDF-Net successfully resolved synoptic-scale dynamic processes, including stratosphere–troposphere exchange events. Based on these findings, HDF-Net can effectively retrieve O3 profiles, thus providing a promising tool for investigating O3 spatiotemporal variability in the Arctic.

title: Bias-Aware Data Quality Enhancement for Forest Fire Detection in AI-Based Remote Sensing
content: Bias, including preconceived notions embedded within the algorithm, selection in dataset collection, imbalances within the training data, impose severe restrictions on the algorithm's performance for accurate detection of forest fires. The variability of forest landscapes, environmental conditions across different geographic regions exacerbates these challenges. Considering the importance of tailored feature extraction, debiasing strategies in improving model performance for critical applications like forest fire detection, a novel approach using dual autoencoder architectures is proposed to independently learn fire-specific, nonfire-specific features. The proposed method effectively addresses visual biases in the datasets, utilizes superimposed image synthesis to create overlay images that enhance feature representation. The proposed integrated framework, validated on the publicly available Wildfire, DFire datasets, shows significant improvements in detection accuracy, robustness. The proposed approach outperforms existing methods with an accuracy of 80%, 76% on the Wildfire, DFire datasets, respectively, achieving lower false positive rates, false negative rates compared to the state-of-the-art methods.

title: Research Bits: Mar. 24
content:
Dual-modulated transistor

Researchers from Daegu Gyeongbuk Institute of Science and Technology (DGIST) and University of Cambridge designed dual-modulated vertically stacked transistors in which two gates, positioned above and below in a sandwich-like structure, control the channel through different mechanisms.

The lower electrode contains microscopic openings to allow electric signals to penetrate deeper into the channel interior, while the upper electrode is made from graphene to enable precise control of current flow. A blocking layer was also used in regions prone to current leakage.

“This research presents a new dual-gate design strategy that enables stable operation even in nanoscale channels,” said Jae Eun Jang, a professor in the Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science at DGIST, in a statement. “By overcoming the fundamental limitations of conventional vertical transistors, the technology is expected to serve as an important solution for accelerating the era of next-generation low-power, highly integrated 3D semiconductors.”

Since the approach doesn’t require ultra-precise alignment or high temperatures, the researchers believe it can be scaled to large-area or multilayer stacked structures. [1]

Graphene FET biosensors

Researchers at Penn State designed graphene FETs for biosensors that perform well in liquid-rich environments, with improved sensitivity and reduced signal drift compared with comparable transistor designs.

“We adjusted the design to have two gates rather than one, allowing us to have independent control over the amount of current flowing through the system,” said Vinay Kammarchedu, an electrical engineering doctoral candidate at Penn State, in a press release. “Using two gates, we can keep the current running through the system constant, removing a primary cause of signal drift. On top of that, we added a feedback system to one of the gates to more accurately track the impact that molecules have on the sensor’s voltage.”

The top gate has 10 times the capacitance of the bottom gate, which amplifies signals coming through the transistor and increases the sensor’s overall responsiveness. “If there is a tiny chemical change in the charge at the sensor’s surface, we see it multiplied by 10 in our measurements due to this feedback system. This allows us to clearly see very minor changes in chemical readings,” Kammarchedu added. “We can integrate up to 32 sensors and measure each one independently without electrical interference. By stacking arrays of these circuit boards together, we can scale up the number of sensors in a system, all while keeping the sensors themselves very small.”

The team is optimizing the sensors to identify volatile organic compounds associated with Parkinson’s disease and will continue to develop the sensing architecture for commercial use. [2]

More robust printed transistors

Researchers at Argonne National Laboratory and Brookhaven National Laboratory are working to improve aerosol jet printing and ink formulations to make longer-lasting, low-power flexible printed electronics.

The ink is based on vanadium dioxide, and redox gating is used to control the flow of electricity. In tests, the printed transistors operated at voltages as low as 0.4 to 0.5 volts and kept working for more than 6,000 on-off cycles. The switches could change states in about one second.

​“Redox gating is robust and does not damage the materials, so we can run thousands of cycles without issues. In previous methods, devices could only run a few times — sometimes just 10 cycles — before failing. Our devices can run thousands of cycles with no problem,” said Wei Chen, a chemist from Argonne and the University of Chicago, in a statement. “From my perspective, the next step is logic devices. We’ve been in contact with industry partners interested in testing our devices for logic applications.” [3]

References

[1] G. Pyo, S. J. Heo, J. Jang, et al. “Dual-Modulated Vertically Stacked Transistors With Fully Laminated Plate-Type Architecture Featuring Nanoscale Channel Length.” Advanced Science (2026): e19410. https://doi.org/10.1002/advs.202519410

[2] V. Kammarchedu, H. Asgharian, H. Chenani, A. Ebrahimi. Active dual-gated graphene transistors for low-noise, drift-stable, and tunable chemical sensing. npj 2D Mater Appl (2026). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41699-026-00674-5

[3] A. J. Erwin, S. Hu, H. Zhou, et al. “Tunable 3D Aerosol Jet Printing of Low-Power Redox-Gated Transistors with Multicomponent Inks.” Adv. Mater. Technol.10, no. 19 (2025): e00648. https://doi.org/10.1002/admt.202500648
